coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescence probe for rapid and sensitive detection of tetracyclines residues based on silicon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhenping
Hou, Jingzhou
Wang, Xianfeng
Hou, Changjun
Ji, Zhong
He, Qiang
Huo, Danqun
coredata.dc:description: A novel rapid and sensitive fluorescence probe based on silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) fluorescence was fabricated for selective detection of tetracyclines (TCs) residues. Si QDs were innovatively prepared via facile One-Pot Solvent-Free Method and characterized by TEM, FT-IR, UV absorption, fluorescence, XPS and XRD. In aqueous solution, Cu2+ and Si QDs complexed together and the fluorescence of Si QDs quenched (static quenching) to a certain extent. TCs can be early in binding to Cu2+ and prevent Si QDs fluorescence quenching. As a consequence, quantitative screening of TCs can be achieved. The assay is highly selective for TCs. Represented by chlortetracycline (CTC), a member of TCs, under optimized conditions, good linear relationship in the range of 11.32–1086.72 nM was obtained, and the detection limit (LOD; S/N ratio = 3) of this assay for CTC is 0.92 nM. It was successfully applied to the determination of CTC in spiked bee honey and total TCs in actual honey samples. And the evaluation of selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the probe were favorable. These results demonstrated that the presented fluorescent probe can be a promising sensing platform for TCs analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Tetracyclines
Fluorescence probe
Chlortetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the sensitivity of the probe
Determination of CTC and total TCs in actual honey samples
The measurement of fluorescence QY for Si QDs
Possible response mechanism of the fluorescence probe
Optimization of detection conditions for CTC
Fluorescent response property of the Si QDs toward CTC
Selectivity of the Si QDs fluorescence probe for TCs detection
The total TCs recognised by Si QDs fluorescence probe
Recovery of CTC and detection of TCs in actual honey samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University ( 2018CDYJSY0055 ), Youth project of science and technology research program of Chongqing Education Commission of China (No. KJQN201804703 ), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2019CDYGZD007 ), Sichuan Key Laboratory of China for Meat Processing (No. 17-S-06 ), Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Project , Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University and the sharing fund of Chongqing University 's large equipment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) and (B) were TEM image, the embedded pictures were interlayer lattice spacing and diameter distribution. (C) EDS pattern, the embedded picture was the elemental ratios (weight and atom percentages) calculated by the EDX software (K shell intensity ratios are indicated). (D) XRD spectrum of the prepared Si QDs showing the characteristic (111), (220) and (311) reflections of diamond structure Si.

(A) FT-IR spectrum of the Si QDs. (B) UV–vis absorption (blue curve), FL excitation (black curve), and emission (red curve) spectra of the aqueous dispersion of the Si QDs. (C) Fluorescent emission spectra of Si QDs at different excitations.
High resolution XPS spectra of the Si QDs: (A) full range, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) O 1s, (E) Si 2p, respectively.

(A) The fluorescence lifetime curves of both Si QDs and Si QDs/Cu2+. (B) The absorption spectrums of Cu2+ (black curve), Si QDs (red curve) and their mixture (blue curve). (C) The zeta potential value of Si QDs and the mixture of Cu2+ and Si QDs solution. (D) Quenching of Si QDs under different conditions.

(A) Relationship between fluorescence quenching of Si QDs and the concentration of Cu2+. (B) The fluorescence quenching curve of Si QDs with the time of TCs and Cu2+ reaction (black curve). The change in fluorescence of pure Si QDs from 0 to 80 min (blue curve). (C) The effect in the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs by pH.

(A) Fluorescence response of the Si QDs upon various concentrations of CTC. The insets of a and b show the photos of the Si QDs in the absence and presence Cu2+ under exciting light of 364 nm, c shows the protective effect of TCs complexation with Cu2+ on fluorescence quenching of Si QDs. (B) F-F0 of the Si QDs as a function of concentration of CTC, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the Si QDs quenched by Cu2+ in the absence of CTC, F is the fluorescence intensity of the Si QDs quenched by Cu2+ which was partially chelated by CTC. Error bars are the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) Relative fluorescence intensity of the Si QDs fluorescence probe in the presence of various antibiotics (the concentration of each substance was 800 nM which is five times as much as CTC). (B) Fluorescence intensity signals obtained for a set of samples (single, binary, ternary and quaternary mixtures of CTC, TC, OTC and DC). (Black column) Intensity of fluorescence obtained by summing the intensities of the single analytes and (red column) intensity of fluorescence experimentally measured.
Schematic diagram of detection of TCs by Si QDs.
The linear range and detection limit of different assay methods for TCs.
Recoveries of TC from spiked honey sample detected by the Si QDs fluorescence probe (n = 3).
Comparison of TCs detection results in real positive honey samples between this probe and ELISA (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescence probe for rapid and sensitive detection of tetracyclines residues based on silicon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biorheological Science and Technology of Ministry of Education
State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Vascular Implants

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors made equal contrition to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel rapid and sensitive fluorescence probe based on silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) fluorescence was fabricated for selective detection of tetracyclines (TCs) residues. Si QDs were innovatively prepared via facile One-Pot Solvent-Free Method and characterized by TEM, FT-IR, UV absorption, fluorescence, XPS and XRD. In aqueous solution, Cu2+ and Si QDs complexed together and the fluorescence of Si QDs quenched (static quenching) to a certain extent. TCs can be early in binding to Cu2+ and prevent Si QDs fluorescence quenching. As a consequence, quantitative screening of TCs can be achieved. The assay is highly selective for TCs. Represented by chlortetracycline (CTC), a member of TCs, under optimized conditions, good linear relationship in the range of 11.32–1086.72 nM was obtained, and the detection limit (LOD; S/N ratio = 3) of this assay for CTC is 0.92 nM. It was successfully applied to the determination of CTC in spiked bee honey and total TCs in actual honey samples. And the evaluation of selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the probe were favorable. These results demonstrated that the presented fluorescent probe can be a promising sensing platform for TCs analysis.

Schematic diagram of detection of tetracyclines (TCs) by silicon quantum dots (Si QDs): In aqueous solution, Cu2+ and Si QDs complexed together and the fluorescence of Si QDs quenched (static quenching) to a certain extent. Tetracyclines (TCs) can be competitive in binding to Cu2+ and prevent Si QDs fluorescence quenching. As a consequence, quantitative screening of TCs can be achieved. The assay is highly selective for TCs.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), a broad antibacterial spectrum and bacteriostatic activity indicated for against many bacterial infections nearly all Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [1,2], which were discovered in the mid-1900s [3]. As a member of TCs, tetracycline has been one of the most commonly used antibiotics in human and veterinary medicine for >75 years [4]. TCs not only have been widely used in the prevention and treatment of animal diseases due to broad spectrum activity and low cost, but also have been reported to be used as growth promoters [5,6]. However, the excessive and improper use in veterinary, TCs are extensively found in various food products including milk, meat, honey, fish, eggs and shrimp [7–10], which cause serious side effects such as liver injury, tooth yellowing, bacterial resistances, allergic reactions and intestinal flora disorder [11–13]. Therefore, the residues of TCs are a major food safety problem faced by human beings, which puts forward higher requirements for the establishment of a rapid and sensitive method for the detection of TCs in food.

Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [14], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,15,16], chemiluminescence [17], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [18], colorimetric methods [19,20], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [21], have been established to monitor TCs. These methods are quite sensitive and accurate, but some of them are expensive and complex process, and always require heavy use of harmful organic solvents, which limit their application for rapid, in-suit and real-time determination. Fluorescence signal-based have been demonstrated to be effective and feasible ways for rapid detection of targets [22–25].

Recently, analytical methods based on water-soluble quantum dots (QDs) as markers of fluorescence aroused extensive interest [26]. Compared with traditional organic dyes, the advantages of QDs are obvious, including wide and continuous excitation spectrum, high luminescence efficiency, high photochemical stability, narrow and symmetric emission spectrum, and tunable color [27]. They were used in various analytes, such as microorganism [28], biomacromolecule [29,30], inorganic molecules and metal ion [31,32]. QDs are also reported to be used to detect tetracycline antibiotics, such as carbon dots derived from rose flowers for tetracycline sensing [33], a quantum dot-based immunoassay for screening of tetracyclines in bovine muscle [34].

In this study, a novel fluorescence probe was designed for rapid and sensitive detection of TCs residues. It is based on silicon quantum dots (Si QDs), which were synthesized innovatively by facile one-pot aqueous method using diaminosilane as silicon source and citric acid as reducing agent, respectively. The resulting Si QDs exhibited good thermostability, strong photostability, excellent salt stability and water solubility. The fluorescence of Si QDs can be quenched by Cu2+ ions based static quenching. TCs can complexate with Cu2+ ions via coordinate bond to avoid fluorescence quenching of Si QDs [35–37], and TCs are identified by the fluorescence probe selectively (Scheme 1 ). Then a selective, sensitive and rapid method for TCs analysis was developed with wide linear range and low detection limit. The fluorescence probe had been successfully applied to the determination of CTC in spiked bee honey and the total TCs in actual honey samples.

Diaminosilane (purity > 98%), CuCl2 (purity > 97%), light petroleum (purity > 98%) and citric acid (purity > 99%) were purchased from Chengdu Kelon chemical reagent factory (Chengdu, China). Tetracycline (TC, purity > 99%), Chlortetracycline (CTC, purity > 99%), oxytetracycline (OTC, purity > 99%) and doxycycline (DC, purity > 99%) standard products purchased from Chongqing Baoguang Biotechnology CO., Ltd (Chongqing, China). Purified water which was from a Molecular water purification system purchased from Chongqing Molecular Water System CO., Ltd (Chongqing, China) with a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ/cm was used to prepare all solutions. PBS purchased from Beijing DingGuo ChangSheng biotech CO., Ltd (Beijing, China).

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–visible) absorption spectra were collected with a TU-1901 UV–visible Spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General instrument Co., Ltd, China). Fluorescence measurements were performed with a LS-55 luminescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Ultrasonic equipment model KQ-700DB purchased from Kunshan ultrasonic instruments CO., Ltd (Kunshan, China). pH values were measured by a Model pH-S20K meter (Mettler Toledo Ltd, Switzerland). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by a Tecnai F20 (FEI, American). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Fourier transform infrared (IR) spectra were acquired on a Bruker vetex70 FT-IR Spectrometer. Particle size and zeta potential were measured by Zeta Sizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). All experiments were carried out at 25 ± 2 °C.

Si QDs were synthesized by reported methods for reference with Minor modifications [38]. Briefly, 20 mL diaminosilane was added to 100 mL flask, deaerated with nitrogen for 5 min, heated in oil bath and strongly stirred at 260 °C. 1 g anhydrous citric acid was quickly added to the solution, reaction quickly, a large amount of smoke, then the mixture was stored at 260 °C for 1 min, and the products was purified with petroleum ether and repeated three times, Si QDs were prepared.

Sample solution to be tested and Cu2+ solution were added to phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.3), dilute to 2700 μL with PBS and interaction for 40 min, selected concentration of Si QDs (300 μL) were added to the each above solution. The measurements were carried out with excitation wavelength at 364 nm and fluorescence spectra were obtained. The scan speed was 1000 nm·min−1 and excitation and emission slits were all set as 10 nm.

In order to obtain a more sensitive fluorescence probe, the concentration of Cu2+, the interaction time between the target and Cu2+, the concentration of Si QDs and pH value for probe system were optimized. Combined with the range of LS-55 luminescence spectrophotometer, after repeated experiments, 106 times diluted original solution was confirmed as the final concentration of probe system for Si QDs. 300 μL diluted Si QDs was mixed with different concentrations of Cu2+ and diluted to 3 mL, the final concentration of Cu2+ was from 0.125 to 3.25 μM. Then the fluorescence intensity of the solutions was measured at 364 nm excitation wavelength. 1.6 μM Cu2+ reacted with 271.68 nM CTC for 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80 min, respectively, and then Si QDs were added to measure the fluorescence intensity immediately. The Si QDs fluorescence intensity in water was measured in the wide pH range of 4–11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the sensitivity of the probe
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of CTC were mixed with 120 μL Cu2+ solution (40 μM), and dilute to 2700 μL. After interaction for 40 min at room temperature, 300 μL Si QDs diluted solution (105 times) was added to each above mixed solution. The final concentrations of CTC were 0–1086.72 nM. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of each solution was measured.

The selectivity of the designed analytical method was investigated in the presence of 135.84 nM CTC, TC, chloramphenicol (CAP), streptomycin (STR), nitrofurazone (NFO), clindamycin (CLI), furazolidone (KAN), furazolidone (FUR) and ciprofloxacin (CIP), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CTC and total TCs in actual honey samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practicability of fluorescent probe in actual samples, various concentrations (0–1086.72 nM) of CTC were spiked into honey samples and the concentrations of CTC were measured. Total TCs in real positive honey samples were detected and the results were compared with ELISA.

The HRTEM image was used for confirming the morphologies and size of the as-prepared Si QDs. Fig. 1A and B showed the Si QDs obtained were well dispersed in the spherical shape with average grain diameter of about 2.6 nm, and the results were consistent with the literature report [39]. The size distribution histogram of the as-prepared Si QDs was calculated with 100 particles from TEM image (inset in Fig. 1B). The high resolution TEM images (inset in Fig. 1A) showed that Si QDs possessed the crystalline structure with an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.22 nm. Fig. 1C depicted EDS pattern of the Si QDs, manifesting that the Si QDs contained Si, C, N and O elements [40], the embedded picture was the elemental ratios (weight and atom percentages) calculated by the EDX software (K shell intensity ratios are indicated), Si, O, and N of 1.16, 4.10, and 3.6% wt concentration, respectively. The carbon content of silicon quantum dots synthesized with organosilicon as silicon source is relatively high [41]. The Mg and F atoms may have originated from the copper grids. Besides, the typical XRD pattern of the Si QDs showing the characteristic diffraction peaks of diamond structure Si (Fig. 1D), further confirms that the nanoscrystals are crystalline Si, well consistent with previous reports [38,42–44].

The surface functional groups of the Si QDs were analysed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 2A). The wide peak at 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the OH and NH stretching vibration [45,46]. The absorption peak at 2976 cm−1 was due to the CH bond antisymmetric stretching vibration [47]. The strong signal at 1651 and 1413 cm−1 indicated the existence of CO and CO stretching vibration, respectively [48]. The absorption band at 1325 cm−1 was attributed to the deformation vibration of OH [49], and the enhanced absorption at 1265 cm−1 was assigned to the bending vibration of C-NH [50]. The absorption band at 1049 cm−1 was due to the SiC asymmetric deformation, the absorption band at 1087 cm−1 was related to the Si-O-Si stretching vibration and the peak at 748–802 cm−1 was result from the wagging vibration of NH [39,51]. The signal around 879 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of SiH bond [52]. Above results showed that the surface of Si QDs was rich in amino and hydroxyl groups which make Si QDs more stable and soluble in water. Good water solubility of Si QDs could provide advantage for the interaction with Cu2+.

The finding of UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum for Si QDs was shown in Fig. 2B. There were two absorption peaks in the UV-absorption spectra of the Si QDs. The absorption band at 285 nm vested in π-π* transition of the CC bond which may be formed by the microwave irradiation process [53]. Another broad absorption band around 358 nm was attributed to the transition of n-π* of the Si QDs [54]. The fluorescence emission peak of the Si QDs was observed at 464 nm under excited at 364 nm. The aqueous solution of the Si QDs exhibited blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. The excitation wavelength of the Si QDs was optimized to obtain better analytical performance. As shown in Fig. 2C, under different excitation wavelengths in the range of 310–400 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelength of Si QDs is nearly excitation-independent. This was consistent with previous literature reports [44,55], and it was another proof that the quantum dots synthesized in this study were Si QDs.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured for the surface composition and elemental analysis of the synthetised Si QDs. It can be seen from Fig. 3A that four major peaks at 101.2, 285.5, 399.8, and 531.2 eV, which were assigned to Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively [39,51]. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s in Fig. 3B exhibited five main peaks at 283.9, 284.5, 285.7, 287.7and 288.6 eV, which could be ascribed to CSi, C–C/C=C, CN, CO, and CO, respectively [39,46,56]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) displayed three peaks centered at 399.2, 400.0 and 401.3 eV, which were attributed to the NSi, C-N-C and N-(C)3 [39,46,51]. As shown in Fig. 3D, O 1s could be divided into three peaks at 531.2, 532.3 and 535.4 eV, corresponding to CO and SiO, respectively [39,46,56]. The three fitted peaks at 101.0, 101.7, and 102.5 eV in the Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 3E) were ascribed to SiC, SiN, and SiO groups, respectively [39,51]. In summary, the surface constitution of the Si QDs measured by XPS was consistent with the FT-IR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The measurement of fluorescence QY for Si QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the method reported [57,58], the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Si QDs was measured by comparative method. Quinine sulfate was used as the fluorescence standard reference substance. The fluorescence QY at the excitation wavelength of 364 nm of Si QDs was 54% (n = 1.33, solvent 0.10 M quinine sulfate). Si QDs fluorescence QY can be calculated by the following formula:
QY sam
=
QY ref
I sam
I ref
A ref
A sam
n sam 2
n ref 2
The QY
sam and QY
ref represent the fluorescence QY of Si QDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. The I
sam and I
ref were the peak area of the emission intensity of the quinine sulfate and Si QDs. The A
sam and A
ref were the ultraviolet absorption value at the excitation wavelength of quinine sulfate and Si QDs, and n was used for representing the refractive index of the corresponding solvent.

The fluorescence QY of Si QDs measured and calculated according the formula was as high as 53.00%. The diaminosilane which was used as silicon source in the synthesis of Si QDs is rich in nitrogen atoms, resulting in a much higher QY than other silicon quantum dots synthesized from 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane or 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of the fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching caused by the reaction of fluorescent molecules with quencher is usually divided into static and dynamic quenching [60]. Fluorescence lifetime is one of the important methods to distinguish dynamic and static quenching. Therefore the fluorescence decay traces of Si QDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ was estimated. As shown in Fig. 4A, fluorescence lifetime curves of both Si QDs and Si QDs/Cu2+ fitted a double-exponential function with average lifetimes of 5.78 and 5.97 ns, respectively. The consistency of its fluorescence lifetime was in accordance with the characteristics of static quenching. Static quenching took place by the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex or coordination between Si QDs and Cu2+ [61]. Comparing the absorption spectrums of Cu2+ (black curve), Si QDs (red curve) and their mixture (blue curve) in Fig. 4B, the absorption spectrum of mixture did not coincide with the simple combinations of Cu2+ and Si QDs. It indicated the formation of stable metal ion-QDs complexes. When combined with the above results, the fluorescence quenching of the Si QDs might be attributed to static quenching. The zeta potential value was also measured, and the Si QDs solution was −13.6 mV, the mixture of Cu2+ and Si QDs solution was −14.3 mV (Fig. 4C). Therefore, Cu2+ mainly interacted with amino groups and shielded part of the positive charge, making the zeta potential lower [62]. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of the Si QDs was attributed to static quenching.

It is well known that TCs which is different from most other antibiotics can complexate with Cu2+ [35,36]. The concentration ratio of Cu2+ to TCs affects the type of complex substance formed. In a neutral solution, TCs occur primarily as zwitterionic and negatively charged forms, the 1:2, 1:1 and 2:1 complex of Cu2+-TCs were produced respectively as the ratio of Cu2+ concentration increases. The complexation sites of TCs with Cu2+ probably are the oxygen atom of enolform hydroxyl group at site 3 and the nitrogen atom of dimethylamino group, the oxygen atoms of hydroxyl group at site 12 and carbonyl group at site 11, or the oxygen atoms of hydroxyl group at site 10 and carbonyl group at site 11, thereby forming at least five speciations [63]. According to the above study reports, the concentration of Cu2+ acting with TC was relatively high in this study, so Cu2+-TCs belonged to a relatively stable type 2:1 complex.

In this study, TCs complexate with Cu2+ early to avoid fluorescence quenching of Si QDs. As shown in Fig. 4D, the black and brown curves are the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs before and after adding quencher Cu2+, respectively, TCs had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs (red curve), the addition of TCs to the quenched Si QDs by Cu2+ does not restore fluorescence intensity (purple curve), the quenching effect of Cu2+ to Si QDs was weakened after interaction with TCs (130 nm) firstly (green curve). Based on the above experimental data, the quenching of Si QDs fluorescence caused by Cu2+ depends on the sequence of action between Cu2+ and Si QDs or TCs. TCs protected against Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching, and Cu2+ complexed with TCs already can't quench Si QDs fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for CTC
ce:section.ce:para: The detection conditions were optimized for obtain a high sensitivity. The original solution of Si QDs was diluted for 106 times as the final concentration of probe system. Such as Fig. 5A shown that with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence quenching of Si QDs becomes more significant, until the Cu2+ concentration reaches 1.6 μM, the quenching effect is gradually stable, so 1.6 μM was selected as the final concentration of Cu2+ in the probe system. As shown in Fig. 5B, within 40 min, with the prolongation of reaction time about TCs and Cu2+, the fluorescence quenching of Si QDs increased gradually, when the reaction time reached 40 min, the degree of fluorescence quenching reached the maximum and tended to be stable (black curve). The change in fluorescence of pure Si QDs from 0 to 80 min was stable (blue curve in Fig. 5B). Therefore, 40 min was used as the reaction time of TCs and Cu2+. The pH value was an important parameter for probe system. The effect in the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs by pH was shown in Fig. 5C, Si QDs in water maintained strong fluorescence intensity in the wide pH range of 4–11. Considering the poor stability of Cu2+in alkaline solution, 0.01 M PBS (pH = 7.3) was selected in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response property of the Si QDs toward CTC
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the detection performance of the system was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 6A, measured according to the method under 2.6, with the increasing of CTC concentration, the fluorescence recovery was becoming more and more obvious. Fig. 6B showed a significant linear relationship between the fluorescence recovery efficiency FF0 and the concentration of CTC. The linear regression equation was (FF0) = 0.2612CCTC + 3.566, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997 and a linear range of 11.32–1086.72 nM. Additionally, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.92 nM based on 3σ/k (σ is the standard deviation of the control, and k is the slope of the calibration curve). Recently, there have been many reports on the detection methods of TCs were listed in Table 1 . Compared with these methods, our study is environment-friendly, timesaving, wide linear range, portable and easy operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the Si QDs fluorescence probe for TCs detection
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is essential for the reliability of analytical methods. Fig. 7A indicated that the relative fluorescence of Si QDs toward CTC was significantly more than other drugs, including chloramphenicol (CAP), streptomycin (STR), nitrofurazone (NOF), clindamycin (CLI), kanamycin (KAN), furazolidone (FUR) and ciprofloxacin (CIP). CTC, TC, OTC and DC are members of the same class of tetracyclines antibiotics which are of similar properties. As shown in Fig. 7B, there is a similar response in the Si QDs fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The total TCs recognised by Si QDs fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The Si QDs fluorescence probe was developed to identify the existence of total TCs in the samples. Only the positive response samples should be further analysed. In order to actually evaluate whether the fluorescence signals from the different individual TCs were in fact additive, set of aqueous samples containing only a single type of TCs as well as binary, ternary and quaternary mixtures of the TCs under study were prepared and analysed by measuring fluorescence signals. In all the cases the solutions assayed contain 135.84 nM of CTC, TC, OTC, DC or their mixture. The samples were analysed by the general procedure described before and results are shown in Fig. 7B. As can be seen, there was a good agreement between the fluorescence intensities of the samples prepared by mixing different TCs and the expected results obtained by adding the intensities of the samples containing a single antibiotic. This indicated the analytical potential of the fluorescence probe for the development of a screening system of TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of CTC and detection of TCs in actual honey samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility and applicability of the proposed Si QDs fluorescence probe were further investigated by measuring spiked honey samples. CTC was spiked into the honey samples at various concentrations and treated for measurement. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of CTC from spiked honey samples ranged from 92.7% to 107.4% and the RSDs ranged from 0.89% to 5.22%. Therefore, the data obtained in this research indicated that the Si QDs fluorescence probe can be an effective approach for accurate detection of TCs in practical samples with high reliability and precision. The novel fluorescence probe was also used to detect TCs in actual honey samples. The results of the detection of the fluorescence probe were consistent with the ELISA in Table 3 . The accuracy of this method is further proved.

To summarize, the water-soluble Si QDs were synthesized via facile One-Pot Solvent-Free Method and an accurate analytical probe was designed to detect CTC, based on the protective effect of the target on the fluorescence of Si QDs in the presence of Cu2+. The performance of the designed fluorescence probe did not require any enzyme and it needed only 1 h for its completion. The Si QDs fluorescence probe which enabled the selective detection of tetracyclines with a detection limit of 0.92 nM for CTC represents many advantage characteristics, such as environment-friendly, timesaving, wide linear range, portable and easy operation. Besides, the novel analytical method can recognise the total TCs and was successfully used to detect TCs in actual honey sample.

Zhenping Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - review & editing. Jingzhou Hou: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xianfeng Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Changjun Hou: Supervision. Zhong Ji: Software, Validation. Qiang He: Writing - review & editing. Danqun Huo: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (2018CDYJSY0055), Youth project of science and technology research program of Chongqing Education Commission of China (No. KJQN201804703), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2019CDYGZD007), Sichuan Key Laboratory of China for Meat Processing (No. 17-S-06), Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Project, Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University and the sharing fund of Chongqing University's large equipment.
